data.prompt_text: activities for a 45 min seminar on using AI critically in lesson planning, digital competence course

suggestions.content: Think-pair-share (20 min): pairs prompt an AI tool for a lesson introduction on their topic, then annotate the output — green for "keep", yellow for "revise", red for "reject with reason" — and defend their annotations to another pair.
suggestions.note: Responding to: "activities for a 45 min seminar on using AI critically in lesson planning, digital competence course"

suggestions.content: Fishbowl discussion (30 min): half the group observes while the other half debates the claim "an AI-written lesson plan is still my lesson plan if I approve it". Observers note which arguments appeal to responsibility, which to efficiency.

Deconstruction exercise (25 min): give students a deliberately mediocre AI-generated activity. Task: identify the three pedagogical assumptions it silently makes, then redesign it for their actual pupil group.

data.prompt_text: learning objectives for the same seminar

suggestions.content: Student teachers can critically evaluate an AI-generated lesson plan against curriculum goals (Lgr22) and justify which parts they would keep, revise, or reject.
suggestions.note: Responding to: "learning objectives for the same seminar"

suggestions.content: Student teachers can articulate the difference between delegating a task to AI and delegating the judgment about that task, using examples from their own subject area.
Student teachers can design a classroom activity in which pupils compare an AI explanation with a textbook explanation and identify the epistemic differences.

data.note: Same three-in-a-minute pattern as last time. This time I actually read the third one properly because I remembered flagging that I'd skimmed it before - it's fine, but noticing that I only read it carefully BECAUSE I knew I'd get asked about it later is a strange feeling.

data.prompt_text: readings for the students, nothing too long

suggestions.content: Skolverket guidance on digital tools in teaching (latest edition) — assign the section on professional responsibility as pre-reading.
suggestions.note: Responding to: "readings for the students, nothing too long"

suggestions.content: A curated set of three AI outputs on the same topic from different models, prepared in advance, so the comparison activity does not depend on live tools.
UNESCO (2023) Guidance for generative AI in education and research — chapter on human agency, as a seminar text.

Student teachers can design a classroom activity in which pupils compare an AI explanation with a textbook explanation and identify the epistemic differences.

data.prompt_text: short rationale paragraph for why this seminar matters, digital competence course

suggestions.content: Consider opening with a concrete dilemma from practice: a colleague uses AI to write all parent communication. What is gained, what is at risk, and who is accountable?
suggestions.note: Responding to: "short rationale paragraph for why this seminar matters, digital competence course"

suggestions.content: A short reflective writing prompt: "Describe one moment this term when you overrode a technology default for a pedagogical reason."
Link this section explicitly to the TPACK framework: which knowledge domain is doing the work here — technological, pedagogical, or content — and where do they intersect?
